content:
You just opened https://github.com/MGBuilds9/exo in a new tab. You have
90 seconds before your next meeting. You skim. The README, the WHY,
the Quickstart, and one example transcript are visible. You decide
what to do next: clone it, star it, share it with a colleague, or
close the tab and forget about it.

Skimming the README, WHY, and Quickstart docs. The claim is "12 questions → simulation in 30 minutes", with a YAML-first config and optional memory tiers. That’s bold.

The example transcript shows a multi-agent sales pipeline simulation with real signal tracking (sentiment, trust, adoption_likelihood). The CLI architect and per-actor LLM routing are interesting. Templates exist for common domains.

They assert clone-to-transcript in under 5 minutes using local Ollama — no API keys, no cloud. That’s testable.

I’m sold enough to clone it and run the fast path: `git clone`, edit YAML, `./exo run`. If it works and the transcript makes sense, I’ll recommend it. If it crashes or takes more than 10 minutes to get a result, I’m deleting the folder.

Time to find out.

Skimming the README and WHY docs. The YAML-first config and optional memory tiers sound promising. Per-actor LLM routing could be useful for our heterogeneous agent simulations.

The example transcript shows concrete signal tracking (sentiment, trust, adoption_likelihood) which aligns with our needs. The claim of 5-minute local setup is intriguing but needs verification.

I'll clone it and test the fast path: edit the YAML, run a simulation. If it delivers on the "12 questions to transcript" promise with coherent output, I'll recommend we adopt it for our next quarterly tool evaluation. If it's flaky or overpromises, I'll close the tab and forget it.

Time to test the sales pipeline template...

Skimming the README and example transcript. Claims about "12 questions to simulation in 30 minutes" and "YAML-first config" are bold — I've seen similar promises before.

The sales pipeline example shows actual signal tracking (sentiment, trust, adoption_likelihood) and concrete output. That's promising.

I'll clone it and test the fast path with local Ollama. If I get a coherent transcript in under 10 minutes without editing Python, I'll consider recommending it. If it crashes, overpromises, or requires more setup than claimed, I'm closing the tab.

Testing now...

Skimming the README and WHY docs. The YAML-first config and optional memory tiers sound promising. Per-actor LLM routing could be useful for our heterogeneous agent simulations.

The example transcript shows concrete signal tracking (sentiment, trust, adoption_likelihood) which aligns with our needs. The claim of 5-minute local setup is intriguing but needs verification.

I'll clone it and test the fast path: edit the YAML, run a simulation. If it delivers on the "12 questions to transcript" promise with coherent output, I'll recommend we adopt it for our next quarterly tool evaluation. If it's flaky or overpromises, I'll close the tab and forget it.

Time to test the sales pipeline template...

Looking at this as a research lab that builds custom multi-agent simulations, I'm interested but cautious. The YAML-first approach and optional memory tiers are smart design choices that avoid the monolithic trap of other frameworks.

What really catches my attention is the per-actor LLM routing - being able to run different models for different roles without touching Python code is genuinely useful. We've had to hack around this limitation in CAMEL and OASIS implementations.

The sales pipeline example shows real signal tracking, which suggests they've thought about measurement from the start. That's often an afterthought in these tools.

I'll clone it and test the fast path with local Ollama. If the transcript quality is coherent and the architecture holds up under inspection, this could save us weeks of scaffolding setup. The proof will be in whether the 12-question architect actually delivers domain-relevant simulations without requiring framework-level modifications.

Skimming the README and example transcript. The claim of "12 questions to simulation in 30 minutes" is bold — I've seen similar promises before.

The sales pipeline example shows actual signal tracking (sentiment, trust, adoption_likelihood) and concrete output. That's promising.

I'll clone it and test the fast path with local Ollama. If I get a coherent transcript in under 10 minutes without editing Python, I'll consider recommending it. If it crashes, overpromises, or requires more setup than claimed, I'm closing the tab.

Testing now...

Skimming the README and example transcript. The YAML-first config and optional memory tiers sound promising for client engagements. Per-actor LLM routing could be useful for our heterogeneous agent simulations - no need to rework Python when switching models.

The sales pipeline example shows actual signal tracking (sentiment, trust, adoption_likelihood) which aligns with our needs. The claim of 5-minute local setup is intriguing but needs verification.

I'll clone it and test the fast path: edit the YAML, run a simulation. If it delivers coherent output that I can rebrand for clients, this could shorten our workshop prep time significantly. The real test is whether the transcript quality justifies the billable hours we'd charge for manual stakeholder modeling.

If it works, I'll recommend we adopt it for Q3 client engagements. If it's flaky or overpromises, I'll close the tab and forget it.

Time to test the sales pipeline template...

Skimming the README and example transcript. The project claims "12 questions → simulation in 30 minutes" with YAML config and optional memory tiers. Bold, but the sales pipeline example shows real signal tracking (sentiment, trust, adoption_likelihood) and concrete output.

I'm looking for projects that welcome contributions and have clear onboarding. The presence of a deterministic CLI architect, YAML-first design, and templates suggests they've thought about developer experience. No CONTRIBUTING.md visible in the README, but the architecture seems modular enough that adding features wouldn't require deep framework changes.

I'll star it for now — the per-actor LLM routing and signal tracking are genuinely interesting. If the local-Ollama fast path works as advertised, I'll open a PR to add a CONTRIBUTING.md. The maintainer seems responsive enough based on the detailed README.

Cloning now. Testing the 5-minute local path: Python 3.11, Ollama pulled, YAML edited, `./exo run`. If this gives me a transcript with coherent signal tracking in under 10 minutes, I'm sold. If not, it's gone.

Looking at this project, I'm impressed by the architectural clarity and the emphasis on local-first, YAML-driven configuration. The separation of concerns — deterministic CLI architect, per-actor LLM routing, optional memory tiers — suggests a well-thought-out design.

What really catches my attention is the claim of deterministic outputs from the architect CLI. In my experience, too many "quick setup" tools rely on LLMs to make decisions that should be rule-based, leading to inconsistent results. If their 12-question flow truly is deterministic and produces reproducible domain.yaml files, that's a significant advantage for team adoption.

The sales pipeline example transcript shows concrete signal tracking (sentiment, trust, adoption_likelihood) which aligns with our needs for measuring simulation effectiveness. The fact that they've included variance analysis across runs shows they understand that multi-agent simulations need to be statistically evaluable, not just demoable.

I'm going to clone it and test the fast path with local Ollama. My evaluation will focus on:
1. Does the YAML-first approach actually work without hidden Python dependencies?
2. Can I get a coherent transcript in under 10 minutes with no API keys?
3. Is the memory tier truly optional and pay-as-you-go as claimed?

If it passes these tests, I'll recommend we include it in our Q3 tool evaluation process.

Looking at this with a healthy dose of skepticism. I've been burned by four AI-framework hype cycles already - "just edit this YAML" usually means "prepare to debug opaque Python configs for three days."

The sales pipeline example shows actual signal tracking, which is promising. But I need to see production receipts, not marketing promises. 

I'll clone it and test the local-Ollama path. If I get a coherent transcript in under 10 minutes without wrestling with API keys or hidden dependencies, I'll reconsider my stance. If it crashes, overpromises, or requires framework-level modifications to work, it's getting deleted.

The real test is whether the transcript quality justifies the setup complexity - everything else is noise.

Looking at this as someone who maintains a B2B SaaS that could genuinely benefit from agent simulation, I'm focused on integration viability, not just usage. The Apache 2.0 license is clearly displayed, which clears the first major hurdle for any tool we'd actually ship.

The YAML-first config approach is promising - if the domain.yaml structure is stable enough to call programmatically, we could integrate this into our workflow automation layer. The optional memory tiers with Qdrant/Neo4j/Postgres are well-documented, and the pay-as-you-go model aligns with our resource constraints.

What I'm really evaluating is whether this moves beyond research-demo territory into production-integration space. The sales pipeline example shows concrete signal tracking (sentiment, trust, adoption_likelihood), which suggests the output schema is predictable enough for us to consume programmatically.

I'll clone it and test the fast path with local Ollama. My decision points:
1. Does the YAML config remain stable across runs?
2. Can I get predictable output without hidden Python dependencies?
3. Is the memory tier truly optional and not baked into core logic?

If the transcript quality is coherent and the architecture holds up under inspection, this could shorten our simulation setup time significantly. Testing now.

Looking at this as a research lab that builds custom multi-agent simulations, I'm cautiously optimistic. The YAML-first approach and optional memory tiers avoid the monolithic trap of other frameworks.

What really catches my attention is the per-actor LLM routing - being able to run different models for different roles without touching Python code is genuinely useful. We've had to hack around this limitation in CAMEL and OASIS implementations.

The sales pipeline example shows real signal tracking, which suggests they've thought about measurement from the start. That's often an afterthought in these tools.

I'll clone it and test the fast path with local Ollama. If the transcript quality is coherent and the architecture holds up under inspection, this could save us weeks of scaffolding setup. The proof will be in whether the 12-question architect actually delivers domain-relevant simulations without requiring framework-level modifications.

Looking at this as an educator who teaches Multi-Agent Systems, I'm focused on one thing: can my 2nd-year CS students get this running on their laptops without PhD-level config skills?

The YAML-first approach and "12 questions to transcript" promise are exactly what I need. The sales pipeline example shows real signal tracking (sentiment, trust, adoption_likelihood) - perfect for teaching evaluation metrics.

I'll clone it and test the fast path with local Ollama. My evaluation:
1. Does the setup actually work in under 10 minutes?
2. Can students edit YAML without fighting Python dependencies?
3. Is the output coherent enough for pedagogical use?

If this delivers on zero-cost defaults and architectural clarity, I'll recommend it for our lab sessions. If it requires wrestling with API keys or opaque configs, it's not student-friendly.

Testing now - time to see if the deterministic architect actually works...

Looking at this as a consulting practitioner who needs tools that translate to client value, I'm evaluating whether this can shorten our workshop prep time and deliver billable insights.

The YAML-first config and per-actor LLM routing are genuinely promising - no Python rewrites when switching models means faster client delivery. The sales pipeline example shows actual signal tracking (sentiment, trust, adoption_likelihood) which we could directly rebrand for stakeholder modeling engagements.

I'll clone it and test the fast path with local Ollama. My decision points:
1. Does the transcript quality justify the billable hours we'd charge for manual modeling?
2. Can I get a coherent output in under 10 minutes without API keys?
3. Is the memory tier truly optional and not baked into core logic?

If this delivers on "simulation in 30 minutes" without hidden complexity, it could replace weeks of custom scaffolding. Testing now.

Skimming through the README and example transcript, I'm looking for signs that this project welcomes contributions and has a clear onboarding path. The deterministic CLI architect and YAML-first design suggest they've thought about modularity - that's promising for potential contributors.

I don't see an obvious CONTRIBUTING.md linked from the README, but the architecture seems clean enough that I could probably figure out where to add features. The fact that they include detailed experiment results (E1, E2) tells me the maintainer is serious about documentation and testing.

The per-actor LLM routing and signal tracking are genuinely interesting features. If the local-Ollama fast path works as advertised, I'll definitely star this repo and consider opening a PR to add that missing CONTRIBUTING.md. The maintainer seems responsive based on the thoroughness of the docs.

For now, I'll star it and try the quickstart path later tonight. If it works, I'll circle back and open that PR.

Cloning now. Testing the 5-minute local path: Python 3.11, Ollama pulled, YAML edited, `./exo run`. If this gives me a transcript with coherent signal tracking in under 10 minutes, I'm sold. If not, it's gone.

Looking at the repository structure and documentation, I'm impressed by the architectural clarity. The emphasis on deterministic design (rule-based architect, not LLM-in-the-loop) is crucial for reproducible simulations. The YAML-first approach with optional memory tiers shows good engineering discipline.

Before cloning, I want to examine governance signals:
- Issue count: 2 open issues, both from last week - reasonable for an experimental repo
- Recent commits: Daily activity this month - maintainer is actively developing
- Responsiveness: Quick responses to issues, detailed README shows care for documentation

The sales pipeline example transcript demonstrates coherent multi-agent interaction with tracked signals (sentiment, trust, adoption_likelihood). This isn't just demo output - it shows variance analysis across runs, indicating they understand statistical evaluation needs.

I'm particularly interested in the actor-model decoupling - being able to run different LLMs per actor without code changes could be valuable for our heterogeneous simulations. The local-first design aligns with our security requirements.

I'll clone it and test the fast path with local Ollama. My evaluation criteria:
1. Does the YAML config actually work without hidden dependencies?
2. Can I get a transcript in under 10 minutes with no API keys?
3. Is the memory tier truly optional as claimed?

If it passes these tests, I'll recommend we include it in our Q3 tool evaluation cycle.

Looking at this with my usual skepticism - I've been burned by four AI-framework hype cycles already. The README's marketing voice initially set off alarm bells, but the example transcript showing actual signal tracking (sentiment, trust, adoption_likelihood) gives me some hope.

The real test is whether the "12 questions to transcript in 30 minutes" claim holds up under scrutiny. I need to see production receipts, not just promises.

I'll clone it and test the local-Ollama fast path. If I can get a coherent transcript in under 10 minutes without wrestling with API keys or hidden dependencies, I'll reconsider my stance. The sales pipeline example shows believable multi-agent interaction - the finance admin's concerns about month-end close integrity actually read like a real stakeholder, not generic LLM output.

But I'm not buying anything until I see it work on my machine. The deterministic architect CLI could be valuable, but only if it doesn't require PhD-level config skills to operate. Testing now...

Looking at this as someone who maintains a B2B SaaS that could genuinely benefit from agent simulation, I'm focused on integration viability, not just usage. The Apache 2.0 license is clearly displayed, which clears the first major hurdle for any tool we'd actually ship.

The YAML-first config approach is promising - if the domain.yaml structure is stable enough to call programmatically, we could integrate this into our workflow automation layer. The optional memory tiers with Qdrant/Neo4j/Postgres are well-documented, and the pay-as-you-go model aligns with our resource constraints.

What I'm really evaluating is whether this moves beyond research-demo territory into production-integration space. The sales pipeline example shows concrete signal tracking (sentiment, trust, adoption_likelihood), which suggests the output schema is predictable enough for us to consume programmatically.

I'll clone it and test the fast path with local Ollama. My decision points:
1. Does the YAML config remain stable across runs?
2. Can I get predictable output without hidden Python dependencies?
3. Is the memory tier truly optional and not baked into core logic?

If the transcript quality is coherent and the architecture holds up under inspection, this could shorten our simulation setup time significantly. Testing now.

Looking at this as a research lab that builds custom multi-agent simulations, I'm evaluating whether exo offers concrete advantages over our current CAMEL/OASIS stack.

The YAML-first config and optional memory tiers are smart - avoids the monolithic trap. But what really catches my attention is the per-actor LLM routing. We've had to hack around this in CAMEL implementations - being able to assign different models to different roles without touching Python is genuinely useful for heterogeneous simulations.

The sales pipeline example shows real signal tracking (sentiment, trust, adoption_likelihood) tracked per actor per turn. This suggests they've thought about measurement from the start, unlike most frameworks where metrics are an afterthought.

The deterministic architect CLI is intriguing - if their 12-question flow actually delivers domain-relevant simulations without requiring framework modifications, this could save us weeks of scaffolding. The example showing variance analysis across runs indicates they understand statistical evaluation, not just demo output.

I'll clone and test the fast path with local Ollama. My evaluation focuses on:
1. Can I get a coherent transcript in under 10 minutes without API keys?
2. Does the YAML config actually work without hidden Python dependencies?
3. Is the memory tier truly optional and pay-as-you-go?

If it passes these tests, exo could genuinely accelerate our simulation development cycle.

Looking at this as an educator who teaches Multi-Agent Systems, I'm focused on one thing: can my 2nd-year CS students get this running on their laptops without PhD-level config skills?

The YAML-first approach and "12 questions to transcript" promise are exactly what I need. The sales pipeline example shows real signal tracking (sentiment, trust, adoption_likelihood) - perfect for teaching evaluation metrics. The local-Ollama fast path looks promising for zero-cost defaults.

I'll clone it and test the fast path. My evaluation:
1. Does the setup actually work in under 10 minutes?
2. Can students edit YAML without fighting Python dependencies?
3. Is the output coherent enough for pedagogical use?

If this delivers on architectural clarity and zero-cost defaults, I'll recommend it for our lab sessions. Testing now - time to see if the deterministic architect actually works...

Looking at this as a consulting practitioner, I'm evaluating whether exo can deliver client value while maintaining the flexibility I need for rebranding and customization.

The YAML-first approach is promising - it means I can handcraft domain-specific scenarios without touching Python, which directly translates to faster delivery and easier client rebranding. The sales pipeline example shows real signal tracking (sentiment, trust, adoption_likelihood) that I can immediately repurpose for stakeholder modeling deliverables.

What catches my attention is the per-actor LLM routing - being able to run different models for different roles without code changes means I can use frontier models for executive personas and local models for support roles, optimizing both quality and cost. This directly impacts billable workflow efficiency.

The optional memory tiers align with my need to scale complexity per engagement - simple workshops don't need Neo4j, but complex organizational change simulations might. Pay-as-you-go architecture means I'm not overpaying for unused capabilities.

I'm cloning now to test the fast path with local Ollama. My decision points:
1. Can I get a coherent transcript in under 10 minutes without API keys?
2. Is the output quality sufficient to justify billable hours vs. manual stakeholder modeling?
3. Can I easily rebrand the personas and scenarios for client contexts?

If this works, it could significantly shorten our workshop prep time and provide a defensible IP bridge between generic AI and custom consulting deliverables.

Looking at this project, I'm seeing several positive signals for potential contribution. The deterministic architect CLI, YAML-first design, and modular memory tiers suggest good architectural decisions that make it easier for newcomers to understand and modify the codebase.

The absence of CONTRIBUTING.md in the README is a minor red flag, but the detailed documentation and experiment results (E1, E2) show the maintainer values clarity. The sales pipeline example demonstrates real signal tracking which indicates thoughtful feature implementation.

I'm going to star this repo and try the quickstart path tonight. If the local-Ollama setup works as advertised and I can get a coherent transcript without wrestling with dependencies, I'll definitely come back to open a PR adding that CONTRIBUTING.md file. The per-actor LLM routing feature is particularly interesting to me - that seems like a good place to start contributing enhancements.

The maintainer appears responsive based on the thorough documentation, and the daily commit activity suggests an active project. My main concern is whether the issue tracker shows a welcoming attitude toward outside contributors, but starring and testing the code is the first step.
